title: DETERMINING THE ROLE OF LYSINE ACETYLATION, IN RNA BINDING PROTEIN-REGULATED, ALTERNATIVE PRE-MRNA SPLICING
author: Nunez, Christopher
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Alternative splicing is the regulated process of splicing that results in different mature mRNA isoforms from the same premature mRNA, thus creating different protein isoforms. The process of alternative splicing is regulated in part by RNA binding proteins that bind to specific sequences on the pre-mRNA and dictate the inclusion or exclusion of regulated exons. Thus, chemical modifications on RNA binding proteins can alter their function and in turn, influence the mRNA composition and resultant protein isoforms.    Recent studies highlight the prevalence of acetyl modifications in RNA binding proteins that function as splicing regulators. This raises the notion that acetylation may play a role in alternative splicing regulation. In this thesis study, we used the well-characterized polypyrimidine tract binding protein to test this hypothesis. We created acetyllysine mimic glutamine and deacetyllysine mimic arginine mutants and assayed the mutant proteins for splicing activity in vivo with three regulated exons.    We conducted bioinformatics analysis to probe the conservation of lysine residues (that are acetylated) across species. Our results highlight, for the first time, a role for lysine acetylation in alternative pre-mRNA splicing activity.
